title: (1) how to build the output of verilator --cc and more generally (2) why is verilator generating makefiles at all?
body:
This post came from an email conversation I had with wsnyder who requested that I post my question here.  There are two parts: (1) specifically, how do I build the output of verilator --cc (without also using --exe) and more generally (2) why is verilator generating makefiles anyway?

The Verilator --exe flag generates a makefile you can use to build your project, but my project is complex enough that I cannot figure out how to give verilator the right arguments such that it generates a makefile that builds my project.  I finally got my build to work using the --cc flag without using the --exe flag by doing this to build the output of verilator --cc:

`VK_OBJS=verilated.o make -C $< -f Vfoo_v.mk`

[ However this method has now stopped working.  When compiling the output of verilator to C++, I now get this:`Vfoo.cpp:54719:15: error: ‘VL_SHIFTR_WWQ’ was not declared in this scope` ]

Evidently this is non-standard, as wsnyder responded as follows below.

> I was confused by this, as there's many more files than verilated.o (VM_SUPPORT_FAST/SLOW) and also VK_OBJS isn't usually done this way.
> Can you comment on what sort of example you are looking for?  --exe is intended to be the simple way, or make a library.

To which I replied as follows:

I found that the makefile constructed by --exe would not build my project.  I concluded it was because I had too many other dependencies that your generated makefile did not know about.  So I ended up building it with [just] --cc and then messing around with the linking until it worked.  The whole experience was very frustrating and took many hours.  Further, I think such a system is perhaps unnecessary.

I know GNU make rather well, but I find the directory and makefile that you generate out of verilog to be quite difficult to work with.  *You* know how to use it, but I think it is not very "discoverable": I have a hard time reading it and figuring out how it works using basic tools.  In particular, when I see a dependency on "FOO", I want to be able to grep for "FOO:" and find where it is satisfied; however, use of generated names and use of VPATH seems to defeat this plan.  Are all those layers of indirection necessary?  Again, they prevent discoverability of the build process.

Might I suggest a reorganization to make the generated makefile more "low powered", that is, just use fewer features of make?  If you are generating both the code and the makefile in the first place, that is a golden opportunity to generate a very simple makefile, rather than generating a makefile that uses further layers of indirection.  Such further layers of indirection are useful for hand-written makefiles, but, again, they reduce discoverability and, if you are generating the makefile anyway, are also unnecessary.

Further, might I suggest thinking of verilator as providing a library to the client, rather than taking over the build process from the user.  That is, I think asking the user to pass in the names of files and flags of their own files so that the verilator-generated makefile can build and link them is a mis-feature.  My files are built in complex ways that your makefile generator cannot anticipate and reproduce.  Why organize it that way at all?  Perhaps I am missing something here, but why not just compile the verilator output to a library against which the client can link?  Again, doing it this way would leave the build process to be managed by the *client*.

*Conclusion:* (1) In the short term, I need my code to start compiling again, so I do need to know how to get verilator to work using --cc.  (2) In the long term, does my suggestion above make sense?  Other compilers do not generate makefiles, they generate code.  Why is verilator generating makefiles at all?  Perhaps it would be simpler for all involved to refactor this design?
